mnemonics.title: Waterfall Phases Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: R-D-I-V-M: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Verification → Maintenance. Memory trick: 'Rani Dances In Very Modern Style' or 'Real Designers Implement Very Methodically'

mnemonics.title: Waterfall Characteristics
mnemonics.content: S-T-A-G-E-D: Sequential, Traditional, Agile-lacking, Document-driven, Extremely rigid, Downward flow only.记忆: 'STAGED' helps remember it's staged/sequential

mnemonics.title: Incremental Model Phases
mnemonics.content: I-D-E-A: Increment → Develop → Evaluate → Adjust.循环: Each increment goes through all SDLC phases

mnemonics.title: Waterfall vs Incremental
mnemonics.content: WATERFALL: Wide rigid flow, all at once, completely documented. INCREMENTAL: Increase gradually, small steps, flexible changes. Rhyme: 'Waterfall falls down fast, Incremental builds that last'

mnemonics.title: When to use Waterfall
mnemonics.content: F-I-X-E-D: Fixed requirements, clear specifications, well-understood domain, exact deliverables. Remember: 'FIXED' = stable requirements needed

mnemonics.title: When to use Incremental
mnemonics.content: C-H-A-N-G-E: Complex projects, High risk, Adaptive requirements, New technology, Growing scope, Evolving needs.记忆: 'CHANGE' is handled well

acronyms.usage: The overall process from conception to deployment. Both Waterfall and Incremental are SDLC models
Document created in Requirements phase of Waterfall. Contains all functional and non-functional requirements
Created in Design phase. Contains system architecture, database design, and technical specifications
Verification (are we building right?) in Implementation; Validation (are we building right product?) in Testing phase
Final testing phase where end-users validate the software. In Waterfall, only at the end; in Incremental, can be done per increment
Breaks project into smaller components. Used in both models for planning, especially useful in Incremental for defining increments

visualTips.visualization: Picture a staircase or fountain flowing downward. Each step must be completed before the next. Imagine water flowing down a waterfall — it only goes one direction, cannot go back up. The phases are like different levels of a building — you must complete each floor before moving to the next

Imagine building with LEGO blocks — each block (increment) adds new functionality. Picture a spiral or circles expanding outward like ripples in water. Think of a tree growing rings — each ring is an increment adding more
Picture a thick stack of papers (documents) flowing down the waterfall. Each phase produces formal documents that become input for the next phase. Heavy documentation = heavy paper stack flowing down

In Waterfall: Customer sees product only at the END (big reveal). In Incremental: Customer sees working software MULTIPLE times (at each delivery). Draw timeline: Waterfall has ONE delivery at end; Incremental has MULTIPLE deliveries
Picture all risks concentrated at the END of the waterfall like a dam. If requirements change late, it's like trying to divert a big waterfall — very difficult and costly
Picture risks spread across the project like drops scattered. Each increment is small, so risks are manageable. Early increments reduce early risks

analogies.analogy: Building a house: You must complete the foundation before walls, walls before roof, roof before painting. Each phase is completely finished before the next begins — just like constructing a house step by step

Following a recipe exactly: If you realize you want less salt after the dish is cooked, you can't go back and fix it. The entire dish must be remade. This represents how changes are costly in Waterfall

Developing a mobile app: First release basic messaging, then add voice calls, then add video, then add file sharing. Each version (increment) adds new features while keeping existing ones — like adding rooms to a house one at a time

Customizing a car: You see and test the base model, provide feedback, then see the next version with modifications. Each iteration gets closer to what the customer wants. In Incremental, customer feedback shapes future increments
Final exam at end of semester: All learning happens first, then one big test at the end. If you fail, you must restart. In Waterfall, testing comes only after everything is built
Quiz-based learning: Small tests throughout the course identify problems early. Each increment has its own testing phase, catching issues early rather than at the end
Architectural blueprints: Before building anything, detailed plans are drawn and approved. Waterfall relies heavily on documentation (blueprints) before implementation begins
Beta versions of software: Companies release beta to get user feedback, then incorporate into next version. Each release is a 'beta' of the next increment
